name: Long Duratón Trail
name_original: Camino del Río Duratón — ida y vuelta por el Parque Natural de las Hoces del Duratón
description: Jog an easy 2.9-mile (4.6 km) route through the Parque Natural de las Hoces del Duratón, spotting vultures and dramatic cliffs.
region_intro: Jogging in Hoces Del Río Duratón offers a unique experience through a landscape shaped by the Duratón River. The region is characterized by deep limestone gorges with cliffs exceeding 100 meters, contrasting with lush riparian vegetation along the riverbanks. Runners can explore diverse ecosystems, from open moorland areas with juniper forests to shaded trails within dense riverside forests of alder, willow, and poplar. This varied terrain provides a dynamic visual and physical experience for all jogging levels. Best jogging routes in Hoces Del Río Duratón The most popular jogging route is Long Duratón Trail, a 2.9 miles (4.6 km) trail that takes 35 minutes to complete. This easy route offers accessible paths with views of the canyon and river. Another top favourite among local runners is Long Trail of the Duratón River – Vultures' Nest loop from Parque Natural de la Hoces del Río Duratón, a difficult 11.7 miles (18.9 km) path. This challenging route leads through the heart of the park, offering opportunities to observe griffon vultures soaring above the dramatic cliffs. Local runners also love the Detour to Spúlveda — circular through the Natural Park Hoces del río Duratón, a 4.5 miles (7.2 km) trail leading through varied terrain and connecting to the historic town of Sepúlveda, often completed in about 51 minutes. Jogging in Hoces Del Río Duratón is defined by limestone gorges, impressive cliffs, and meandering riverbeds, with trails winding through riparian forests and open moorland. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy riverside runs to more challenging routes with elevation changes. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 40 runners have used komoot to explore Hoces Del Río Duratón's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Hoces Del Río Duratón?
A: There are over 15 running routes documented in Hoces Del Río Duratón, catering to various fitness levels. These include easy riverside paths, moderate trails, and more challenging long-distance runs through the gorges.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when jogging in Hoces Del Río Duratón?
A: Jogging in Hoces Del Río Duratón offers a diverse experience. You'll find paths winding through deep limestone gorges with impressive cliffs, alongside lush riparian vegetation, and through open moorland areas with juniper forests. The varied terrain ensures a dynamic visual and physical experience.

Q: Are there any easy running routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, there are easy running options. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1164015682/long-duraton-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Long Duratón Trail&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 2.9-mile (4.6 km) route that offers accessible paths with scenic views of the canyon and river, making it suitable for a relaxed run.

Q: Where can I find challenging running trails in the park?
A: For a more challenging experience, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31451171" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Long Trail of the Duratón River – Vultures' Nest loop from Parque Natural de la Hoces del Río Duratón&lt;/a&gt;. This difficult 11.7-mile (18.9 km) path leads through the heart of the park, offering significant elevation changes and opportunities to observe griffon vultures.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in Hoces Del Río Duratón?
A: Yes, many routes are circular, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1164034373/detour-to-spulveda-circular-through-the-natural-park-hoces-del-rio-duraton" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Detour to Spúlveda — circular through the Natural Park Hoces del río Duratón&lt;/a&gt;, a 4.5-mile (7.2 km) trail that connects to the historic town of Sepúlveda.

Q: What natural attractions or wildlife can I see while running?
A: Hoces Del Río Duratón is a Special Protection Area for Birds, so you're likely to see griffon vultures, Egyptian vultures, and golden eagles soaring above the canyon. The park also features dramatic limestone gorges, the meandering Duratón River, and diverse flora. Keep an eye out for otters and wild boars near the riverbanks.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or points of interest along the running trails?
A: Absolutely. You can encounter fascinating historical sites such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6204193" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ermita de San Frutos&lt;/a&gt;, an ancient hermitage perched on a promontory with panoramic views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5229115" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cave of the Seven Altars&lt;/a&gt;, a rupestrian sanctuary, also offers a glimpse into the region's past.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about the trails in Hoces Del Río Duratón?
A: Runners frequently praise the breathtaking natural beauty, especially the dramatic cliffs and the chance to observe griffon vultures. The variety of terrain, from shaded riverside paths to open viewpoints, and the well-maintained trails are also highly appreciated by the komoot community.

Q: Is it possible to combine a run with a visit to a nearby town?
A: Yes, the medieval town of Sepúlveda serves as a gateway to the park and offers access to many routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20142100" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sepúlveda – Vulture Cliff loop from Sepúlveda&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging option that starts from the town, allowing you to explore its charm before or after your run.

Q: Are there good viewpoints along the running routes?
A: Many trails offer spectacular viewpoints. The area around the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3706411" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vulture Cliff&lt;/a&gt; is renowned for its panoramic vistas of the canyon and the opportunity to see vultures up close. The promontory where the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6204193" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ermita de San Frutos&lt;/a&gt; is located also provides stunning views over a meander of the Duratón River.

Q: Are the running routes in Hoces Del Río Duratón suitable for dogs?
A: While many natural parks in Spain allow dogs on trails, it's always recommended to keep them on a leash to protect wildlife, especially in a Special Protection Area for Birds like Hoces Del Río Duratón. Always check local regulations for specific routes before heading out with your pet.

Q: Where can I park when visiting the running trails?
A: Parking is generally available near popular trailheads and access points to the natural park. Towns like Sepúlveda, which act as gateways to the park, typically offer parking facilities. For specific routes, such as those near the Puente de Villaseca, designated parking areas are usually provided.
tips:
The Duratón canyon is a valley nestled between the plateau of the Segovian páramo with walls that reach one hundred meters in several areas and an average width of fifty meters. The road draws the river line of the canyon, it is winding and undulating between imposing limestone and dolomite walls that have suffered a forceful wear of karstic dissolution for millions of years. At first the water filtered into the earth, forming an underground water course below the current course and due to erosion the roof and the side walls collapsed and the current bed emerged, although much narrower. Later periglacial modeling continued to affect the cuts in the gorges and increased the width of the gorge until the gorges became what they are today. The bottom of the river valley is an alluvial plain of gravel, sand, silt and clay where forest species grow that fill the interior of the gorge with life and color up to the meanders of the Burgomillodo reservoir, in the final stretch of the river within of the space of the natural park. In the central part of the gorges is the mouth of the Valdemuelas stream, a narrow gorge that descends from Villar de Sobrepeña, the area where the largest community of griffon vultures and birds of prey in the natural park is concentrated.

The path is a constant gallery of poplars, poplars, alders, ash trees, willows, elms and other forest species associated with water courses. In some meanders the plain is more extensive and there are old plantations of walnut, chestnut, plum and wild hazelnut trees, the autumn festival of color is guaranteed. More than five hundred pairs of griffon vultures nest on the walls of the gorges, there are also Egyptian vultures, golden eagles and peregrine falcons.
Fantastic place to see the vultures, as it is not a protected area, it allows you to see them much closer than other known areas of the Hoces del Duratón.

Thanks to this simple construction, since time immemorial, the inhabitants near the town of Sepúlveda have been able to cross the river and approach the other side of the riverbed that crosses close to the town.

faqs.question: Is this jogging trail suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Camino del Río Duratón — round trip is classified as an easy jogging route. It covers approximately 4.62 km with an 80-meter elevation gain and is suitable for any fitness level, thanks to its mostly paved surfaces. The nearby Senda de la Molinilla is also a very simple and practically flat option, ideal for families.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this jogging route?
faqs.answer: This jogging route is designed to be completed in approximately 35 minutes, covering a distance of about 4.62 km. This duration is based on a steady jogging pace.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail?
faqs.answer: While specific parking locations for this exact trailhead are not detailed, the Parque Natural de las Hoces del Duratón generally offers various access points. It's advisable to research parking options near Sepúlveda or other common entry points to the park, as many routes, including this one, can be accessed from different areas.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this jogging route?
faqs.answer: The terrain along this route is varied but generally accessible. Many sections within the park, including parts of this trail, feature mostly paved surfaces, making it easy for jogging. Other areas may have varied terrain, but paths are generally well-maintained. Some paths within the larger park can include narrow sections along vertical walls or slanted rocks, which might be treacherous when wet, so always be mindful of conditions.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife can I expect to see along the way?
faqs.answer: The Parque Natural de las Hoces del Duratón is a Special Protection Area for Birds (ZEPA), so you can expect excellent birdwatching opportunities. It's home to one of Europe's largest colonies of griffon vultures, which you can often see soaring above the dramatic cliffs. You might also spot Egyptian vultures, golden eagles, and peregrine falcons. The riverbanks also host diverse flora.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: This route takes you through the stunning Hoces del Duratón Natural Park, known for its dramatic limestone cliffs and gorges. You'll also pass by the impressive Vulture Cliff, a prime spot for birdwatching, and cross the historic Talcano Bridge. The area also features ancient caves and the Romanesque Hermitage of San Frutos.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to jog this route?
faqs.answer: Autumn is particularly picturesque for jogging this route, as the riverbanks are adorned with vibrant foliage colors from poplars, willows, and ash trees. However, the park offers stunning scenery year-round. Consider the weather conditions, as some paths can be treacherous when wet.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Camino del Río Duratón jogging trail?
faqs.answer: Regulations for dogs within the Parque Natural de las Hoces del Duratón can vary. While many natural parks in Spain allow dogs on a leash, it's always best to check the most current local regulations for the specific area you plan to visit to ensure compliance and protect the local wildlife.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for the Parque Natural de las Hoces del Duratón?
faqs.answer: Generally, there is no entrance fee to access the Parque Natural de las Hoces del Duratón for activities like jogging. However, certain activities or access to specific protected areas within the park might require permits, especially during peak seasons or for larger groups. It's advisable to check the official park website for any specific regulations regarding permits for your visit.

faqs.question: Does this trail connect with other routes in the park?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route is part of the larger network of trails within the Parque Natural de las Hoces del Duratón. It fully covers the Senda Larga del Duratón and also intersects with sections of the Senda de los Dos Ríos and the Puente Talcano trail.

faqs.question: What should I wear for jogging this trail, especially in autumn?
faqs.answer: For jogging this trail, especially in autumn, wear comfortable, moisture-wicking athletic clothing. Layers are recommended as temperatures can change. Sturdy jogging shoes with good grip are essential, particularly if paths might be wet or uneven in sections. Don't forget water, sun protection, and perhaps binoculars for birdwatching.
